coredata.dc:title: Ultra-fast solvent-free protocol remodels the large-scale synthesis of carbon dots for nucleolus-targeting and white light-emitting diodes
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Xin
Hu, Jinshuang
Wei, Hua
Li, Zihan
Liu, Jian
Zhang, Jie
Yang, Shenghong
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) provides unprecedented opportunities for optical applications due its unique properties, but the energy-extensive consumption, high-risk factor and time-consuming synthesis procedure greatly hinders its industrialization process. Herein, we proposed an ultra-low energy consumption solvent-free synthetic strategy for fast preparing green/red fluorescence carbon dots (G-/R-CDs) using m-/o-phenylenediamine and primary amine hydrochloride. The involvement of primary amine hydrochloride can improve the formation rate of G-CDs/R-CDs through effectively absorbing microwave energy and providing acid react environment. The developed CDs exhibit good fluorescence efficiency, optical stability and membrane permeability for dexterous bioimaging in vivo. Based on inherently high nitrogen content, the G-CDs/R-CDs possess excellent nuclear/nucleolus targeting ability, and were successfully applied for screening cancer and normal cells. Furthermore, the G-CDs/R-CDs were further applied for fabricating high-safety and high-color rendering index white light-emitting diodes, providing a perfect candidate for indoor lighting. This study opens up new horizons for advancing practical applications of CDs in related fields of biology and optics.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Ultra-fast
Solvent-free
Cancer cell screening
White light-emitting diodes

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of the G-CDs and R-CDs
Intracellular localization of CDs and SYTO RNA-Select
Orderly synthesis of G-CDs and R-CDs
Cellular uptake and nuclear entry mechanism of G-CDs and R-CDs
Comparison of G-CDs/R-CDs and commercial nuclear/nucleolus dyes on living cells imaging
G-CDs/R-CDs for the distinction between normal and cancer cells

Jiangxi Provincial Natural Science Foundation (No. 20224BAB203029 ). Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province (No. ZR2020QE015 ). Science, Education and Industry Integration Innovation Pilot Project from Qilu University of Technology (Shandong Academy of Sciences) (Nos. 2022JBZ02-04 , 2022PY073 , 2022PY061 ).

We acknowledge the financial support from the projects of National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 22106054, 52001178, 21806189). Jiangxi Provincial Natural Science Foundation (No. 20224BAB203029). Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province (No. ZR2020QE015). ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Illustration of the synthetic procedure of G-CDs and R-CDs (★), UV–Visible absorption and fluorescent spectra of G-CDs (A) and R-CDs (E). The TEM images of G-CDs (B) and R-CDs (F), Inset: high-resolution TEM image of G-CDs and R-CDs. The FTIR spectra of G-CDs (C) and R-CDs (G), and High-resolution N1s XPS spectra of G-CDs (D) and R-CDs (H).

Fluorescence imaging of Hela cells treated with G-CDs (A1), Hoechst 33342 (A2, B2) and R-CDs (B1). Merged image of A1 and A2 (A3), B1 and B2 (B3). Fluorescence intensities of the regions in green and blue channel (A4), and in red and blue channel (B4). Fluorescence imaging of Hela cells treated with SYTO RNA Select (C1) and R-CDs (C2). Fluorescence imaging of Hela cells treated with R-CDs (D1) and G-CDs (D2). Merged image of C1 and C2 (C3), D1 and D2 (D3). The intensity profiles of the white line frame across the cell and correlation between both the dyes were analyzed (C4, D4). Scale bar: 20 μm. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Fluorescence imaging of Hela cells stained by G-CDs (A) in DNase (C) and RNase (E) digestion experiments and their fluorescence intensity distribution (A1, C1, E1). Fluorescence imaging of Hela cells stained by R-CDs (B) in DNase (D) and RNase (F) digestion experiments and their fluorescence intensity distribution (B1, D1, F1). Scale bar: 20 μm.

Fluorescence imaging of G-CDs (A1-A5) and R-CDs (B1-B5) incubated Hela cells in the presence of different endocytosis inhibitors. Hela cells internalization percentages of G-CDs/R-CDs in the presence of different endocytosis inhibitors (A6,B6). Scale bar: 20 μm.

Fluorescence imaging of Hela cells stained with G-CDs (A), R-CDs (B), Hoechst 33342 (C) and SYTO RNA Select (D) under continuous irradiation. Anti-photobleaching evaluation of G-CDs, R-CDs, Hoechst 33342 and SYTO RNA Select (E). Scale bar: 20 μm.

Fluorescence imaging of MCF-10A (A), A549 (B) and Hela (C) cell lines stained by G-CDs. Fluorescence imaging of MCF-10A (D), A549 (E) and Hela (F) cell lines stained by R-CDs. Frequency distributions of nucleolus numbers per cell for MCF-10A, A549 and Hela cell stained by G-CDs (G) and R-CDs (H). Scale bar: 20 μm.
Emission spectra (A, B, C) and CIE coordinates (D, E, F) of cold, pure and warm WLEDs (inset: photograph of cold, pure and warm WLEDs).
Ultra-fast solvent-free and large-scale strategy for preparing long emission wavelength CDs. G-CDs/R-CDs are applied for cancer-cells screening and white light-emitting diodes.

head.ce:title: Ultra-fast solvent-free protocol remodels the large-scale synthesis of carbon dots for nucleolus-targeting and white light-emitting diodes

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Qilu University of Technology (Shandong Academy of Sciences), Jinan 250353, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering
Qilu University of Technology (Shandong Academy of Sciences)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Qilu University of Technology (Shandong Academy of Sciences), Jinan 250353, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Advanced Materials, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Jiangxi Normal University, Nanchang 330022, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Advanced Materials, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Jiangxi Normal University, Nanchang 330022, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) provides unprecedented opportunities for optical applications due its unique properties, but the energy-extensive consumption, high-risk factor and time-consuming synthesis procedure greatly hinders its industrialization process. Herein, we proposed an ultra-low energy consumption solvent-free synthetic strategy for fast preparing green/red fluorescence carbon dots (G-/R-CDs) using m-/o-phenylenediamine and primary amine hydrochloride. The involvement of primary amine hydrochloride can improve the formation rate of G-CDs/R-CDs through effectively absorbing microwave energy and providing acid react environment. The developed CDs exhibit good fluorescence efficiency, optical stability and membrane permeability for dexterous bioimaging in vivo. Based on inherently high nitrogen content, the G-CDs/R-CDs possess excellent nuclear/nucleolus targeting ability, and were successfully applied for screening cancer and normal cells. Furthermore, the G-CDs/R-CDs were further applied for fabricating high-safety and high-color rendering index white light-emitting diodes, providing a perfect candidate for indoor lighting. This study opens up new horizons for advancing practical applications of CDs in related fields of biology and optics.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon-based nanomaterials open a brand horizon for the reasonable and high-security application of optical materials in many fields including chemo- and bio-sensing [1], bioimaging [2], photoelectric catalysis [3], photoelectric element [4], diagnostic and therapeutic procedures [5]. Especially, carbon dots (CDs) have been acclaimed as a new carbon-based nanomaterial with great potential following fullerene, carbon-nanotubes, and grapheme etc [6,7]. CDs possess unprecedented application prospects in these fields and may be industrialized in the near future due to its low cost, safe and non–/low-toxic, green and environmentally friendly characteristics [8]. Therefore, the rational design and large-scale synthesis of CDs with perfect fluorescent properties, low cost, and high yield is one of the key scientific issues to promoting CDs from laboratory research to commercial application.

Regrettably, synthesizing high-quality CDs with long emission wavelengths mainly depends on solvent-thermal reaction at high temperature and high pressure, which greatly restricts the industrialization and commercial application of CDs [9–11]. The involvement of solvent, especially flammable, explosive, and toxic organic solvents, not only is unfavorable to environmental protection and green chemistry, but also requires complicated post-processing procedure to discard large amounts of by-products, resulting in unsatisfactory actual output or yield of CDs [12–14]. From this point of view, solvent-free reaction was strongly advocated for mass production of CDs recent years which can solve above problem correctly [15]. Unfortunately, most of presented reports about large-scale synthesis of CDs still cannot get rid of their dependence on high energy consumption and time-consuming originating from high temperature/pressure reactions, which great against safety, cost-effective and large-scale industrial production [16,17]. Therefore, further optimizing the solvent-free protocol for mass synthesizing functional CDs to promote the wide application of CDs is of great value.

The good biocompatibility and excellent optical tunability of CDs provide capacious application prospects in biomedicine and photoelectric element [18]. In biomedicine field, nucleolus is considered to be an ideal target for cancer treatment [19], and nucleolus targeted tracking was deemed to a particularly important means for the pathological diagnosis of malignant lesions and for predicting tumors or other disorders [20,21]. The rise of CDs creates a new opportunity for developing nucleolus targeting probe, which can well solve the disadvantages of commercial probe SYTO RNA Select, including expensive, complicated synthesis and staining procedures, and harsh storage conditions [22]. More importantly, the CDs can conquer the defect of potential cytotoxicity of other types nano-based probes forcefully [23]. Significantly, the CDs with nucleolus targeting ability that can be mass production is still scarce. On the other hand, CDs has become a perfect candidate in photoelectric element field due its tunable optical properties [24–26]. Compared with other fluorescent nanomaterials, such as semiconductor nanocrystals, perovskite quantum dots, and rare earth-based nanomaterials etc., the CDs with low environmental risk provide a strong guarantee for the safety development of green photoelectric elements [27,28]. Therefore, it is significant to realize the fast, low energy consumption, low-cost and large-scale synthesis of CDs on the basis of preserving its functionality.

In this study, we proposed an ultra-low energy consumption solvent-free synthetic strategy for fast and large-scale preparing long emission wavelength CDs to realize reasonable nuclear/nucleolus targeting and white light-emitting diodes (WLEDs) fabrication. Specially, m-/o-phenylenediamine were select as carbon sources considering their excellent optical tunability and abundant nitrogen active sites, while primary amine hydrochlorides were preferred as high-efficient reactants and catalysts. Firstly, high-efficiency green and red fluorescent CDs can be obtained easily in 1 min by microwave pyrolyzing the solid mixture of primary amine hydrochloride and m-phenylenediamine (m-PDA) and o-phenylenediamine (o-PDA), respectively. The quantum yield (QY) of synthesized G-CDs and R-CDs can reach 58.8% and 27.0%, respectively. More importantly, the obtained two types of CDs all exhibit excellent nucleolus targeting performance due to their inherent high nitrogen content. G-CDs and R-CDs also can be used to image Daphnia Magna and zebrafish in vivo. Moreover, cold, pure and warm WLED with high CRI were fabricated based on 450 nm chip coated with G-CDs and R-CDs (Scheme 1 ). The main advantage of this study is the gram-level, fast, green and low-cost synthesis of CDs in the ordinary laboratory and reasonable biomedicine and photoelectric element applications, which is expected to promote the CDs to industrialize and commercialize apply.

SYTO RNA-Select was purchased from Life Technologies (USA). M−Phenylenediamine (99%), o-phenylenediamine (98%), methylamine hydrochloride (98%), ethylamine hydrochloride (98%), propylamine hydrochloride (98%), butylamine hydrochloride (98%), DNA (85%), DNase (≥2,000 Kunitz units/mg protein), Hoechst 33342 (98%), RNA (BR), RNase (≥2,000 units/mg protein), chlorpromazine (95%), methyl-β-cyclodextrin (average Mn 1310) and genistein (97%) were purchased from Aladdin Biological Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All other reagents were used without any further purification. The whole experiment used ultra-pure water with a resistivity of 18.25 MΩ⋅cm (UPR-II-40L, Sichuan, China).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the G-CDs and R-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: In a typical process, 0.40 g of m-PDA and 0.2 g of methylamine hydrochloride (or ethylamine hydrochloride, propylamine hydrochloride, butylamine hydrochloride) were grounded into uniform powders in an agate mortar. Then transfer the powder to a 50 mL beaker. After heated in domestic microwave oven (700 W) for 1 min, the powders changed from white to black, indicating the formation of G-CDs. For synthesizing R-CDs, 0.40 g of o-PDA reacts with 0.05 g of methylamine hydrochloride (or ethylamine hydrochloride, propylamine hydrochloride, butylamine hydrochloride) under same conditions. After cooled down to room temperature, the crude product of R-CDs was firstly dispersed in water and then collected product in alkaline environment to remove impurities initially. The solid products were further dispersed in ethanol and then were centrifuged (8000 r/min, 10 min) and the supernatant liquids were collected and dialyzed (molecular weight cut-off 500). Then the products were freeze-dried for further application.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Intracellular localization of CDs and SYTO RNA-Select
ce:section.ce:para: Prior to staining, cells were rinsed once with PBS, then with R-CDs (5 μg·mL−1, pH = 3.0) for 20 min at 37 °C and rinsed with PBS three times. Then cells were treated with SYTO RNA-Select (500 nM) for another 20 min at 37 °C. Intracellular localization of R-CDs and G-CDs was performed through similar procedures. Hela cells were fixed with 4% paraformaldehyde (PFA) for 15 min and washed three times with PBS to remove the residual PFA. More details are in supporting information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Orderly synthesis of G-CDs and R-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: In order to achieve solvent-free, rapid, and mass-produced preparation of CDs, we proposed a novel microwave-assisted solid-phase synthesis strategy. In this system, m-PDA and o-PDA were selected as effective carbon source and primary amine hydrochloride was used to promote the solid-phase reaction. As shown in Fig.S1, single m-PDA or o-PDA cannot be used to synthesize high PL performance CDs directly. However, the QYs of products can be improved significantly from 0.34% to 58.8% and 0.17% to 27.0% after primary amine hydrochloride involved. The G-CDs and R-CDs can be obtained through the solid phase reaction of R-NH2·HCl (R=-(CH2)n-CH3, n = 0–3) and m-PDA and o-PDA respectively within 5 min, as shown in Fig.S2, and the highest QYs of G-CDs and R-CDs can reach 58.8% and 27.0% under optimal conditions. For one-minute fast preparation, the QYs still can achieve 52.3% and 27.0% for the G-CDs and R-CDs, respectively. The QYs of prepared CDs were shown in Table S1, which suggest that different types of primary amine hydrochloride have little effect on the properties of the products. In this system, the primary amine hydrochloride can effective absorb and conduct microwave energy and provide an acidic environment for effectively accelerating the reaction system to rapidly polymerize and carbonize, and increase efficiency [29,30].

Control experiments were carried out to further explain the advantages of proposed route based on fluorescence QYs, as summarized in Table S2 and S3. From the results, we can conclude following conclusions: Firstly, phenylenediamine and primary amine hydrochloride are all indispensable from reactions G1-4 and R1-4; furthermore, microwave pyrolysis is significantly better than solvothermal or high temperature and pressure reaction from reaction G5-7 and R5-7, which can effective avoid unnecessary consumption of time and energy; and solvent-free is much more beneficial for the synthesis of high performance CDs in this system from G8-9 and R8-9. Compared with previous reports, the QYs of synthesized CDs are higher or comparable to the CDs from other reaction routes (Table S4 and S5), which still possess the advantages of less time-consuming, low energy consumption and solvent-free. Considering the QYs and reaction time, which was described as QY/Time, the efficiencies of proposed G-CDs and R-CDs are superior to previously reported CDs. In particular, the properties of CDs obtained by using different primary amine hydrochlorides to react with m-/o-PDA are similar, and the G-CDs and R-CDs refer the products from the reaction of methylamine hydrochloride with m-PDA and o-PDA, respectively, in the rest of this article.

The G-CDs and R-CDs can emit brightly green (510 nm) and red (600 nm) fluorescence (Fig. 1 A and E) and the emission peaks of G-CDs and R-CDs are almost independent of excitation wavelength (Fig.S3,4). The double-peak emission of R-CDs should be attribute to its unique electronic structure of the carbon core [31]. As shown in Fig.S5, the G-CDs and R-CDs all can exhibit perfect PL performance in common organic solvents. The synthesized G-CDs/R-CDs have high tolerance to temperature, continuous excitation light, and high ionic strength, the fluorescence intensities of proposed CDs varies no more than 10%, which has a significant benefit for biomedical application (Fig.S6).

The properties of four kinds of G-CDs or R-CDs synthesized by the m-PDA/o-PDA and methylamine/ethylamine/propylamine/butylamine hydrochloride are similar, and the products through the reaction of methylamine hydrochloride and m-/o-PDA were as representative to characterize in the following. As shown in UV–Vis absorption spectrum of G-CDs (Fig. 1A), the absorption of 248 nm can be assigned the π-π* transition of aromatic sp2 domains [32], while n-π* transition was reflected at 370 nm [33]. The absorption band centred at 460 nm is associated with the π-state coupling [34]. For R-CDs (Fig. 1E), peaks at 240 nm and 295 nm can be ascribed to the π-π* transition of aromatic sp2 domains of R-CDs. Peaks at 450–630 nm result from the n-π* transitions of the aromatic sp2 [35].

The morphology G-CDs and R-CDs were characterized with TEM. G-CDs (Fig. 1B) and R-CDs (Fig. 1F) showed homogeneous size distribution and good dispersity with an average diameter of 3.5 nm and 4.5 nm from Fig.S7, respectively. The microstructure of G-CDs and R-CDs were characterized by high-resolution TEM, and the lattice spacing of G-CDs and R-CDs is 0.20 nm and 0.18 nm, respectively, as shown in Fig. 1B and F. The small size of developed CDs was benefited for facilitating cellular uptake and intra-cellular transport, which is key factor for subcellular targeting applications [36]. In this work, the fluorescence mechanism of G-CDs and R-CDs mainly depend on the size of the sp2 conjugate domain [37]. The theoretically calculated values are 1.91 eV (G-CDs) and 1.76 eV (R-CDs), which is calculated using Tauc plots. The emission wavelength red-shift of CDs derived from the narrowing of the band gap caused by a larger size. Compared to m-PDA, o-PDA tends to form greater conjugated sp2 hybrid structure, and the R-CDs with larger size show a longer emission wavelength than G-CDs. DFT B3LYP function, 6-31G* basis set are chosen in density functional theory experiment to simulate and calculate the simulation structure of CDs [38]. The calculated band gap of G-CDs and R-CDs is 1.95 and 1.72 eV, respectively, which is corresponding with theoretical results. The possible formation process can be shown in Fig.S8, under the action of primary amine hydrochloride, phenylenediamine is protonated and polymerized to form a dense carbon core structure, and further, a flexible polymer shell is formed under the action of oxygen and high temperature [31].

In Fig.S9, similar to other reported CDs, the XRD patterns of G-CDs and R-CDs exhibit associated diffusion peak at around 23° and 26°, respectively, which are consistent with HRTEM results [39,40]. As shown in FTIR in Fig. 1C and G, both G-CDs and R-CDs possessed abundant oxygen-/nitrogen-containing functional groups, which indicates that oxygen in the air was involved in the reaction. For G-CDs, the absorption peaks at 3155–3670 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibrations of OH and NH, CO (1716 cm−1), CN/CC (1614 cm−1), CN (1463 cm−1), COC/COH (1236 cm−1) [41]. Moreover, the surface of R-CDs (Fig. 1G) exists the vibrations of CN (1390 cm−1), CC (1512 cm−1), CN (1635 cm−1), CO (1705 cm−1), NH (3243 cm−1) and OH (3423 cm−1) [42]. From the XPS spectra, as shown in Fig. S10, there are three main peaks of 284.5 eV (C 1 s), 398.7 eV (N 1 s) and 531.6 eV (O 1 s) can be observed easily in the full spectrum of G-CDs and R-CDs. For G-CDs, the N 1 s suggests the presence of pyridinic N (398.6 eV), pyrrolic N (399.4 eV), and graphitic N (400.4 eV) (Fig. 1D), while the peaks of pyridinic N, pyrrolic N and graphitic N in R-CDs located at 398.4, 399.8, and 400.3 eV, respectively (Fig. 1H). The high-resolution XPS spectrum of C 1 s and O 1 s further proves the existence of CO, CN/CO and CC/CC in prepared CDs [43,44].

A suitable nuclear-targeting probe usually meet two conditions: firstly, smaller particle size (less than 8 nm) can ensure the probe goes through the nuclear pore smoothly into the nuclear; on the other hand, chemical or physical interactions such as groove binding, electrostatic binding or intercalative binding between probe and nucleic acid are necessary [45]. Among them, higher nitrogen-element content (higher than 15%) of probe is much beneficial to combine nuclear/nucleolus [46,47]. The nitrogen contents of synthesized G-CDs and R-CDs can reach 19.95% and 23.91%, respectively, as shown in Table S6, which provide sufficient acting sites for DNA/RNA.

Combing with the advantages of simple synthesis method, smaller particle size and low cost, the proposed CDs are much suitable to be applied in nuclear/nucleolus imaging. Specially, the imaging region of G-CDs/R-CDs can be regulated through changing pH. As shown in Fig.S11, the staining region of the G-CDs/R-CDs in different pH conditions is different, the G-CDs and R-CDs all can specific stained nucleolus with pH of 2–4. When pH is at 6–9, the G-CDs become nuclear targeting. For R-CDs, the nuclear is dyed under pH = 5 and the cytoplasm is stained at pH = 6–9. We speculate that this phenomenon may be ascribed to the surface charge difference between G-CDs and R-CDs. From Fig.S12, we can see the zeta potential (ζ) of the G-CDs is much more positive than that of R-CDs, and the isoelectric point (pI) is about 7.0 and 6.0, respectively, which indicates the obvious staining difference under different pH conditions. As we know, the pI of DNA (4–4.5) is different from RNA (2–2.5) [48], and the DNA has a positive charge while the RNA with negative charge at pH = 3. Therefore, the G-CDs/R-CDs exhibit higher combing capacity to nucleolus with RNA-riched than chromosome with pH of 3.0.

As shown in Fig.S13A and C, the ζ change of G-CDs/R-CDs after RNA added is large than adding DNA when pH = 3, then the G-CDs/R-CDs can be used for specific nucleolus targeting at this condition. Furthermore, when pH is 7 for G-CDs and pH is 5 for R-CDs, the decreased degrees of ζ are similar due the approximate negative charges of DNA and RNA, which reflected in the nuclear targeting in cell imaging (Fig.S13B,D).

The specific nuclear targeting property of the G-CDs/R-CDs was verified through co-staining experiment with commercial nuclear staining dye Hoechst 33342 and G-CDs/R-CDs. In Fig. 2 , the staining area of G-CDs/R-CDs can be identified as the nuclear by combining the fluorescence imaging of Hoechst 33342 and G-CDs/R-CDs, From Fig. 2A4 and B4, we can see that G-CDs/R-CDs and Hoechst 33342 have high overlap rate with Pearson’s coefficient up to 0.97 and 0.95, respectively, which suggest the excellent nuclear targeting ability of prepared CDs.

Commercial nucleolus dye SYTO RNA Select was used as control to further verify the targeting ability of the G-CDs/R-CDs. In Fig. 2C1-C4, R-CDs and SYTO RNA Select show good colocalization capabilities with Pearson’s coefficient of 0.96. And G-CDs also show excellent targeting ability to nucleolus by using R-CDs as reference (Fig. 2D1-D4), and the co-located coefficient can reach 0.99. Therefore, both of the G-CDs/R-CDs exhibit high nucleolus-targeting accuracy. From Fig.S14, we can see that the developed G-CDs/R-CDs also have no obvious stray light while avoiding the interference of short-wavelength fluorescence to biological imaging, thereby showing robust commercial application. Furthermore, the G-CDs/R-CDs also show good biocompatibility, the cell survival rate is over 83% with concentration of 800 μg·mL−1 no matter dark conditions or irradiated by daylight lamp for 2 h (Fig.S15). Besides, the series products of G-CDs/R-CDs 1–4 are all have the above-mentioned nucleolus-targeting capabilities (Fig.S16). All above results further guarantee the practicability of the developed CDs in nuclear/nucleolus targeting imaging.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cellular uptake and nuclear entry mechanism of G-CDs and R-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: To further explore the nucleolus-targeting mechanism of G-CDs/R-CDs, the digestion experiment of Hela cells was carried out. As shown in Fig. 3 A,B, the bright nucleolus is observed in the controlled Hela cells. After treatment with DNase, fluorescence signal and fluorescence distribution of nucleolus were not significantly changed (Fig. 3C,D). Whereas, after RNase treatment, the fluorescence intensity of nucleolus is weakened significantly (Fig. 3E,F), the fluorescence distribution shows significant difference, and the nucleolus almost disappear.

Transmembrane mechanism of nucleolus-targeting behavior was explored by low-temperature method and three inhibitors, including low-temperature treatment (4℃, suppression of energy generation), chlorpromazine (CPZ, clathrin-mediated endocytosis inhibitor), methyl-β-cyclodextrin (MβCD, lipid raft-mediated endocytosis inhibitor) and genistein (GEN, caveolae-mediated endocytosis inhibitor) [49]. The quantitative analysis of fluorescence intensity also shows cell internalization of the G-CDs/R-CDs is almost unrelated from low temperature or various inhibitors (Fig. 4 ), which confirmed that G-CDs/R-CDs have extraordinary cell penetration capacity [50,51].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of G-CDs/R-CDs and commercial nuclear/nucleolus dyes on living cells imaging
ce:section.ce:para: The nuclear/nucleolus targeting performances of G-CDs/R-CDs and commercial dyes were compared through series of experiments to explain the superiority of proposed materials, including light bleaching resistance and staining stability. As shown in Fig. 5 A,B, after the continuous irradiation for 20 min, the fluorescence intensity of the G-CDs/R-CDs still can maintain about 60%, while the fluorescence of Hoechst 33342 and SYTO RNA Select is almost unable to capture after radiation for 10 min and 3 min, respectively (Fig. 5C-E). It is worth noting that G-CDs/R-CDs can stain the nucleolus selectively after a brief incubation of 5 min, as shown in Fig.S17. There is no obvious signal entrapment of lysosomal and endosomal, suggesting that the G-CDs/R-CDs move quickly to the nucleolus after cell internalization. On the other hand, even after dyeing 2 h, the G-CDs/R-CDs is still positioned in nucleolus and has not been transferred to other regions, which indicate that the proposed CDs can be used for long-term and real-time target tracking. Above results indicate that the developed CDs possess better stability than commercial dyes.

The developed G-CDs/R-CDs also exhibit the advantages of low-cost, simple synthesis and good water dispersibility except the better performance than commercial dye. Compared to commercial dyes of Hoechst 33342 and SYTO RNA Select, the cost of G-CDs and R-CDs is much lower. Moreover, the yield of G-CDs and R-CDs is up to 55.3% and 78.1%, suggesting the possibility of large-scale preparation. Therefore, the G-CDs/R-CDs proposed can be an effective alternative over commercial dyes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: G-CDs/R-CDs for the distinction between normal and cancer cells
ce:section.ce:para: The G-CDs and R-CDs possess extremely high targeted precision for the nucleolus, and it can accurately distinguish cancerous cells from normal cells based on number and morphology information [52]. The G-CDs and R-CDs were applied to treat MCF-10A, A549 and Hela cells. From Fig. 6 we can see that the nucleolus of MCF-10A is evenly distributed with a round shape, but the nucleolus shapes of cancer cells are irregular whether A549 or Hela cells. When cells stained with G-CDs, the mean number of nucleolus in MCF-10A, A549, and Hela cells can be calculated as 1.74, 3.41 and 3.62, respectively (Fig. 6G), and using R-CDs, the average number of nucleolus is 1.68, 3.38, and 3.78, respectively (Fig. 6H). These results strongly indicate that the developed G-CDs/R-CDs have excellent screening ability for cancer cells and normal cells, which also have the likelihood of being applied to early cancer diagnosis of cellular levels.

The high fluorescent efficiency and good optical stability of developed CDs also provide new chance for aquatic organism imaging. From Fig.S18 we can see that the prepared G-CDs/R-CDs show outstanding in vivo imaging performance for Daphnia Magna, zebrafish embryo and larva. The G-CDs and R-CDs enter the intestines of the Daphnia Magna, as well as the abdomen of the zebrafish embryo. Interestingly, the G-CDs can infiltrate into nasal cavity of the zebrafish larvae, while the R-CDs is more likely to enter the abdomen of the zebrafish larvae.

Benefiting from the good fluorescence performance of G-CDs/R-CDs in most organic solvents, CDs-based WLEDs devices were tried be fabricated to further broaden the application scenarios of proposed CDs. Using G-CDs/R-CDs as phosphors, working voltage was set as 3 V and 450 nm blue light chip was as base. By adjusting the mass proportion of G-CDs/R-CDs, WLED with adjustable correlated color temperature (CCT) and high color rendering index (CRI) can be produced, including cold-WLED, pure-WLED and warm-WLED. When mass ratio of G-CDs/R-CDs was kept 1.0:15.6, cold WLED was fabricated with CIE coordinates of (0.27,0.31), CCT of 9171 K and CRI of 87.0 (Fig. 7 A,D). Change the mass ratio to 1.0:22.3, a pure WLED with CIE coordinate maintained with (0.33, 0.34) is prepared (Fig. 7B,E), which is similar to sunlight. The CCT of the pure WLED is 6825 K and the CRI is as high as 92.9. When the mass ratio of G-CDs/R-CDs was 1.0:48.2, the device showed warm white light with CIE coordinates of (0.39, 0.35), CCT = 3475 K and CRI = 80.3 (Fig. 7C,F), which is an ideal light source for indoor lighting [53].

In summary, we proposed an effective ultra-low energy consumption solvent-free synthetic strategy for synthesizing functionalized G-CDs/R-CDs, which realized the rapidly and large-scale preparation of that. The proposed G-CDs/R-CDs with the high nitrogen content displays excellent performance nuclear/nucleolus targeting property and were successfully applied for the screening of normal and cancer cells. Compared to commercial dyes, the developed G-CDs/R-CDs possess the advantage of ultra-high yield, low-cost and higher precise positioning, which exhibits promising practical application in biomedical application. Furthermore, the WLEDs devices with adjustable CCT and high CRI (80.3–92.9) also were constructed successfully based on the well maintenance of fluorescence characteristics of G-CDs/R-CDs in epoxy resin. Besides, related research on full-color emissive CDs by ultra-low energy consumption solvent-free synthetic strategy is still in progress. We expect that this study not only can bring new way of vision and thinking to the large-scale production of CDs, but also can facilitate the practical and reasonable application of CDs in various fields.

Xin Wang: Conceptualization, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Jinshuang Hu: Investigation, Methodology. Hua Wei: Software, Investigation. Zihan Li: Data curation, Investigation. Jian Liu: Methodology, Formal analysis. Jie Zhang: Conceptualization, Supervision. Shenghong Yang: Funding acquisition, Visualization, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We acknowledge the financial support from the projects of National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 22106054, 52001178, 21806189). Jiangxi Provincial Natural Science Foundation (No. 20224BAB203029). Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province (No. ZR2020QE015). Science, Education and Industry Integration Innovation Pilot Project from Qilu University of Technology (Shandong Academy of Sciences) (Nos. 2022JBZ02-04, 2022PY073, 2022PY061).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2023.06.171.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
